Mining refers to the act of keeping a record of transactions on a cryptocurrency network, which is also called validation. Another means is to purchase cryptocurrency from another party, which can be done through cryptocurrency exchanges.~

column2: ~What technology is cryptocurrency based on?~
column3: ~Blockchain or Distributed Ledger technology is used to keep records of all users connected to the network.~

column2: ~What is cryptocurrency?~
column3: ~Cryptocurrency is a digital asset that can be traded as a currency issued by the private sector.~

column2: ~Is it allowed to trade Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies in Nepal?~
column3: ~Since Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies are illegal in Nepal and have not yet been legally recognized, such transactions are not allowed. Such transactions are legally illegal.~

column2: ~What are the guidelines for the administration of foreign investment and foreign debt by Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~The administration of foreign investment and foreign debt by Nepal Rastra Bank is guided by the Nepal Rastra Bank Foreign Investment and Foreign Debt Management Regulations, 2078 (including the second amendment).~

column2: ~Where should an application be submitted for a money changer's license?~
column3: ~An application for a money changer's license can be submitted to the appropriate provincial office of Nepal Rastra Bank or the Foreign Exchange Management Department. However, the process of issuing new money changer licenses at locations other than international airports is currently closed.~

column2: ~Can a natural person obtain a license to conduct foreign exchange transactions?~
column3: ~No, natural persons cannot obtain a license to conduct foreign exchange transactions. Only legally established firms, companies, incorporated associations, and organizations can obtain a license.~

column2: ~How much fee should a money changer pay to obtain/renew a license?~
column3: ~A money changer must pay the following fees to obtain/renew a license:

column1: *   Twenty-five thousand rupees for purchase and sale transactions of Indian rupees.
*   One hundred thousand rupees for purchase and sale transactions of convertible foreign currency.
*   One hundred twenty-five thousand rupees for both transactions mentioned above.
*   An additional twenty-five thousand rupees for transactions providing exchange facilities for passports to Nepali citizens traveling abroad.~

column2: ~How much deposit/bank guarantee does a money changer need to submit to obtain/renew a license?~
column3: ~Money changers must submit the following deposit/bank guarantee to obtain/renew their license:

column1: *   One hundred thousand rupees for Indian rupee purchase and sale transactions.
*   Five hundred thousand rupees for convertible foreign currency purchase and sale transactions.
*   Six hundred thousand rupees for both the above transactions.
*   An additional two hundred thousand rupees for transactions that also provide passport exchange facilities to Nepali citizens traveling abroad.

In lieu of the bank guarantee, a cash security equal to the same amount can be deposited in Rastra Bank. Additionally, government agencies, government universities, government educational institutions, and non-for-profit social organizations are not required to submit such a guarantee/cash security.~

column2: ~What is the duration of a license to conduct foreign exchange transactions for money changers?~
column3: ~The duration of a license to conduct foreign exchange transactions for money changers is three years.~

column2: ~What is the number of licensed money changers in Nepal?~
column3: ~There are a total of 369 licensed money changers in Nepal, 161 within the Kathmandu Valley and 208 outside the Kathmandu Valley.~

column2: ~What is the penalty for failure to renew a foreign exchange transaction license on time for travel/trekking, hotels, airlines, cargo and other companies?~
column3: ~Failure to renew a foreign exchange transaction license on time will result in a fine twice the renewal fee for each month for a period of one year from the date of expiry.~

column2: ~What are the procedures for renewing the license for foreign exchange transactions by travel/trekking, hotels, airlines, cargo and other companies?~
column3: ~Travel/trekking, hotels, airlines, cargo and other companies must complete the procedures as mentioned in Chapters 3 and 4 of the Nepal Rastra Bank, Foreign Exchange Transaction Licensing and Inspection Regulations, 2077 to conduct foreign exchange transactions. These regulations are available on the bank's website https://www.nrb.org.np. Additionally, these companies should submit an application for renewal before the expiry of the license.~

column2: ~What is the duration of the license to transact foreign exchange for travel/trekking, hotels, airlines, cargo and other companies?~
column3: ~The duration of the license to transact foreign exchange for travel/trekking, hotels, airlines, cargo and other companies is a maximum of three years.~

column2: ~How much is the annual fee for obtaining and renewing a license to transact foreign exchange by travel/trekking, hotels, airlines, cargo and other (except money changers and remittance companies) organizations?~
column3: ~The annual fees for obtaining and renewing a license to transact foreign exchange are as follows:

column1: *   **Departmental stores/supermarkets, airlines, and hotels of three stars and above:** four thousand rupees only.
*   **Any other hotel, resort, firm, company or institution and government body with the objective of earning profit:** two thousand rupees only.

column2: ~What should travel/trekking, hotel, airline, cargo and other companies do to conduct foreign exchange transactions?~
column3: ~Travel/trekking, hotel, airline, cargo and other companies must complete the procedures as mentioned in Chapters 3 and 4 of the Nepal Rastra Bank, Foreign Exchange Transaction Licensing and Inspection Regulations, 2077 to conduct foreign exchange transactions. The regulations are available on the bank's website https://www.nrb.org.np.~

column2: ~What are the arrangements for bringing remittances into Nepal through digital medium?~
column3: ~Banks, financial institutions, and remittance service provider companies licensed by Nepal Rastra Bank can enter into agreements with Digital Payment institutions operating in countries where Nepalis are employed to allow digital remittances to Nepal. Here are the conditions:

column1: *   The sending institution must be associated with a Digital Payment Instrument operator in the foreign country.
*   The institution must be registered and licensed for Outward Remittance business in that country.
*   Agreements must specify security measures for transactions, including identification, system security, confidentiality, and settlement.
*   Parties must commit to securely maintaining digital transaction records.
*   If a recipient cannot transfer or collect payment, excess amounts in the wallet must be transferred to their bank account.
*   Compliance with AML/CFT regulations is required.
*   Automatic reimbursement arrangements must be in place for incomplete transactions.
*   Agreements must be submitted to Nepal Rastra Bank.
*   Remittance details must be submitted to the Foreign Exchange Management Department on a fortnightly basis.~

column2: ~Can the remitter close his/her business if he/she wants to?~
column3: ~Yes, the remitter can close his/her business if he/she wants to. However, prior approval must be obtained from the Nepal Rastra Bank, Foreign Exchange Management Department.~

column2: ~How much capital is required to obtain a remitter license?~
column3: ~For a remittance company that issues remittance cards, it is Rs. 250 million, for a remitter company that transacts as a principal company, it is Rs. 100 million, and for a company that transacts remittances as an agent of a principal company located abroad, it is Rs. 200 million.~

column2: ~For how long is a remittance business license granted?~
column3: ~A remittance business license is granted for a maximum of one year. Such a license can be renewed.~

column2: ~How many companies are there in Nepal that conduct remittance transactions?~
column3: ~There are 51 licensed companies (excluding banks and financial institutions) that conduct remittance transactions in Nepal.~

column2: ~Can Nepali citizens invest abroad?~
column3: ~Nepali citizens are not allowed to make any kind of investment abroad. However, Nepali citizens living abroad can open a bank account abroad and invest with the foreign exchange earned during their stay abroad or the foreign currency earned from the investment of that foreign exchange.~

column2: ~What documents should be submitted for the exchange approval for repatriating the amount earned from foreign investment through technology transfer?~
column3: ~The following documents should be submitted for the exchange approval for repatriating the amount earned from foreign investment through technology transfer: the technology transfer agreement approved by the regulatory body, the approval letter from the regulatory body for repatriating the amount earned for technology transfer, the invoice issued by the concerned foreign investor, proof of tax paid as per the rules on the amount earned, and the decision of the board of directors of the concerned organization regarding the exchange approval for repatriating the amount earned, the details of the amount earned certified by the auditor, and the latest tax payment and audited financial statements.

column2: ~What is the procedure to be followed to repatriate the amount earned from foreign investment?~
column3: ~To repatriate the amount earned from foreign investment that has already been accounted for, an application must be made to the Single Point Service Center under the Department of Industry or the Foreign Exchange Management Department of Nepal Rastra Bank along with an approval letter from the Department of Industry or the Investment Board.~

column2: ~What documents should be submitted for accounting of foreign investment?~
column3: ~For accounting of foreign investment, the documents mentioned in the notice published by Nepal Rastra Bank on 2073/12/31 should be submitted. The link to the said notice is here.~

column2: ~What documents are required to be submitted for approval of foreign investment?~
column3: ~For approval of foreign currency inflow for foreign investment, the documents mentioned in the notice published by this bank on 2069/10/09 must be submitted. The link to the said notice is <a href="http://archive.nrb.org.np/fxm/notices/FDI_Policy.pdf">here.~

column2: ~Is accounting for investments and loans mandatory? ~
column3: ~Yes, accounting for foreign investment and foreign loans is mandatory to repatriate unaccounted foreign investment and foreign loan proceeds and to purchase and sell shares held in the name of foreign investors.~

column2: ~Where should one apply for permission to import foreign currency for foreign investment and foreign loans?~
column3: ~One should apply to the Foreign Exchange Facilitation Unit under the Foreign Exchange Management Department of Nepal Rastra Bank located at the Single Point Service Center, Department of Industry, Government of Nepal, Tripureshwor.

column2: ~Can Nepali citizens, firms and organizations borrow in foreign currency from their relatives, other individuals, non-resident Nepalis or organizations abroad?~
column3: ~Yes, Nepali citizens, firms and organizations can borrow in foreign currency from their relatives, other individuals, non-resident Nepalis or organizations abroad, interest-free or within the maximum limit of 1 year LIBOR rate + 5.5%, up to a maximum of US$ 1 million and in the case of India, up to 100 million Indian rupees.~

column2: ~Is there any limit set for the interest rate on foreign currency loans taken by firms/companies established in Nepal?~
column3: ~Yes, the interest rate on foreign currency loans taken by firms/companies established in Nepal should be within the maximum limit of 1 Year LIBOR + 5.5%.~

column2: ~Can industries/companies established with foreign investment take loans in foreign currency?~
column3: ~Industries/companies established with foreign investment can take loans in foreign currency in accordance with prevailing legal provisions.~

column2: ~What are the industries or businesses that are not open to foreign investment in Nepal?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, the following industries or businesses are restricted for foreign investment in Nepal:

column1: 1.  Industries or businesses in sectors other than large industries related to animal husbandry, fisheries, beekeeping, fruits, vegetables, oilseeds, pulses, dairy business and primary agricultural products that export at least 75 percent of their production.
2.  Small and home enterprises.
3.  Personal service businesses (such as hair cutting, sewing, driving, etc.)
4.  Industries producing arms, ammunition, explosives or nuclear biological and chemical (NBC), industries producing atomic energy, radioactive materials.
5.  Real estate buying and selling business (except construction industry), retail trade, domestic courier service, local catering service, money changer, remittance service.
6.  Travel agencies involved in tourism, guides, trekking and mountaineering guides, rural tourism including homestays.
7.  Mass media business (newspapers, radio, television and online news), national language film business.
8.  Management, accounting, engineering, legal consulting services and language training, music training, computer training.
9.  Consulting services with more than fifty-one percent foreign investment.~

column2: ~What is the limit for foreign investment in Nepal?~
column3: ~The minimum limit for foreign investment in Nepal is Rs. 20 million per foreign investor, while there is no maximum limit. However, this minimum limit does not apply to non-resident Nepalis (natural persons) investing in Nepal through an investment company established with joint investment by the Government of Nepal and non-resident Nepalis.~

column2: ~What should be done to open new microfinance institutions?~
column3: ~To open a new microfinance institution, one must obtain a license from Nepal Rastra Bank.~

column2: ~Can a microfinance institution collect deposits from the general public?~
column3: ~A microfinance institution is not allowed to collect savings deposits from individuals other than its members. Only limited branches of Nirdhan Utthan Microfinance Institution Limited and Chhimek Microfinance Institution Limited have been granted permission to collect deposits from the general public.~

column2: ~Where are complaints regarding microfinance financial institutions heard?~
column3: ~Complaints regarding microfinance financial institutions are heard by Nepal Rastra Bank. For this, Nepal Rastra Bank has a complaints hearing unit under the Banking and Financial Institutions Regulation Department.~

column2: ~What are the provisions regarding the scope of work for microfinance financial institutions?~
column3: ~There are two scopes for microfinance financial institutions: the provincial level, which allows them to open branches in districts of the same province, and the national level, which allows them to open branches throughout the country.~

column2: ~How many types of microfinance institutions are there?~
column3: ~There are two types of microfinance institutions in Nepal based on their loan transactions: those that deal only with wholesale loans and those that deal with retail loans.~

column2: ~What are the main sources of funds for microfinance institutions?~
column3: ~The main sources of funds for microfinance institutions are the amount received as capital from shareholders, savings collected from members, loans received from banks and financial institutions, and funds allocated from the profits made by the institution.~

column2: ~What is the interest rate and service fee of a microfinance financial institution's loan?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, a microfinance financial institution can charge a maximum interest rate of 15 percent and a service fee of up to 1.50 percent on the loans it disburses.~

column2: ~What is the loan limit of a microfinance financial institution?~
column3: ~A microfinance financial institution can disburse unsecured microloans of up to Rs. 300,000 per member (maximum Rs. 500,000 for members who have availed loans for the past 2 years and are in good standing) and collateralized loans of up to Rs. 700,000 for poor and low-income individuals, firms or groups engaged in specified agriculture, microenterprises and businesses, and up to Rs. 1.5 million for individuals who have availed loans for the past 2 years and are in a good category.~

column2: ~How many types of loans can a microfinance financial institution provide?~
column3: ~A microfinance financial institution can provide two (2) types of loans: unsecured microloans under group guarantee and secured loans against acceptable collateral.~

column2: ~Who regulates and supervises microfinance financial institutions?~
column3: ~Nepal Rastra Bank regulates and supervises microfinance institutions.~

column2: ~What is a microfinance financial institution?~
column3: ~An institution that receives a license from Nepal Rastra Bank and makes poor and low-income individuals and women members, collects regular savings from them, and provides microfinance loans without collateral to operate agricultural and microenterprise businesses on a collective basis, and provides collateral loans to operate microenterprises and microbusinesses by taking acceptable collateral from its members or other persons, and collects savings from members in limited amounts is called a microfinance financial institution.~

column2: ~When was Nepal Rastra Bank established?~
column3: ~Nepal Rastra Bank was established on 26 April 1956 (Baisakh 14, 2013).~

column2: ~Under which act was Nepal Rastra Bank established?~
column3: ~Nepal Rastra Bank was established under the Nepal Rastra Bank Act, 1955.~

column2: ~What is the main responsibility of Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~The mission of Nepal Rastra Bank is to “achieve macroeconomic and financial stability through effective monetary, foreign exchange, financial sector, and payment system policies for sustainable and inclusive economic development.”~

column2: ~What happened to the domestic financial institutions after the establishment of NRB?~
column3: ~After the establishment of Nepal Rastra Bank (NRB) in 1956, there has been significant growth in both the number and the activities of the domestic financial institutions in the country.~

column2: ~Which act redefined the functions and objectives of NRB?~
column3: ~The new Nepal Rastra Bank Act, 2058 (2002), redefined the functions and objectives of NRB.~

column2: ~How does NRB aim to increase public confidence?~
column3: ~NRB aims to increase public confidence towards the banking and financial system by maintaining stability of the banking and financial sectors.~

column2: ~What kind of payment system does NRB aim to develop?~
column3: ~NRB aims to develop a secure, healthy and efficient system of payment.~

column2: ~How does NRB communicate its policies and strategies?~
column3: ~NRB communicates its policies and strategies through its annual monetary policy, which conveys various policies, strategies, and actions.~

column2: ~What is the vision of Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~The vision of Nepal Rastra Bank is to be a "modern, dynamic, reliable and effective central bank".~

column2: ~What is the mission of Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~The mission of Nepal Rastra Bank is to “achieve macroeconomic and financial stability through effective monetary, foreign exchange, financial sector, and payment system policies for sustainable and inclusive economic development.”~

column2: ~Who served as Governor from August 10, 1990 to January 17, 1995?~
column3: ~Mr. Hari Shankar Tripathi served as Governor from August 10, 1990 to January 17, 1995.~

column2: ~When did Mr. Maha Prasad Adhikari’s term end?~
column3: ~Mr. Maha Prasad Adhikari’s term ended on April 5, 2025.~

column2: ~When did Prof. Dr. Biswo Nath Poudel begin his term as Governor?~
column3: ~Prof. Dr. Biswo Nath Poudel began his term as Governor on May 6, 2025.~

column2: ~How many members are there in the Board of Directors of Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~There are seven members in the Board of Directors of Nepal Rastra Bank.~

column2: ~How many of the board members are ex-officio?~
column3: ~Four board members are ex-officio.~

column2: ~How many non-executive Directors are there in the NRB Board?~
column3: ~There are three non-executive Directors in the NRB Board.~

column2: ~Who is the Chairperson of the NRB Board of Directors?~
column3: ~Prof. Dr. Biswo Nath Poudel is the Chairperson / Governor of the NRB Board of Directors.~

column2: ~Who are the four ex-officio members of the NRB Board?~
column3: ~The four ex-officio members of the NRB Board are: the Governor, the Secretary, Government of Nepal (GON), Ministry of Finance, and two Deputy Governors.~

column2: ~Who appoints the Governor and Deputy Governors of NRB?~
column3: ~The Governor and both Deputy Governors are appointed by the GON, Councils of Ministers for a term of five years.~

column2: ~What is the term length for the Governor and Deputy Governors?~
column3: ~The Governor and both Deputy Governors are appointed by GON, Councils of Ministers for a term of five years.

column2: ~Who appoints the three non-executive Directors of NRB?~
column3: ~The GON, Councils of Ministers appoints the three non-executive Directors.~

column2: ~From which fields are the non-executive Directors chosen?~
column3: ~The three non-executive Directors are appointed from amongst persons renowned in the fields of Economics, Monetary, Banking, Finance and Commercial Law.~

column2: ~Who is the current Chairperson of the NRB Board?~
column3: ~Prof. Dr. Biswo Nath Poudel is the current Chairperson / Governor of the NRB Board.~

column2: ~Who represents the Ministry of Finance in the NRB Board?~
column3: ~Mr. Ghanshyam Upadhyaya, Secretary, Ministry of Finance, represents the Ministry of Finance in the NRB Board.~

column2: ~When was Mr. Ghanshyam Upadhyaya appointed as a member?~
column3: ~Mr. Ghanshyam Upadhyaya was appointed as a member on February 13, 2025.~

column2: ~Who are the current Deputy Governors of NRB?~
column3: ~The current Deputy Governors of NRB are: Dr. Neelam Dhungana Timsina and Mr. Bam Bahadur Mishra.~

column2: ~When was Dr. Ravindra Prasad Pandey appointed as a member?~
column3: ~Dr. Ravindra Prasad Pandey was appointed as a member on May 11, 2023.~

column3: ~Dr. Gunakar Bhatta is the Executive Director of the Office of the Governor.~
