column2: ~What is the buying rate of 1 Saudi Arabian Riyal (SAR)?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, the buying rate of 1 Saudi Arabian Riyal (SAR) is 37.56 NPR.~

column2: ~What is the buying rate of 1 Qatari Riyal (QAR)?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, the buying rate of 1 Qatari Riyal (QAR) is 38.67 NPR.~

column2: ~What is the selling rate of 1 Qatari Riyal (QAR)?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, the selling rate of 1 Qatari Riyal (QAR) is 38.83 NPR.~

column2: ~ What does RTGS system membership require?~
column3: ~RTGS system membership is open to entities that meet the following criteria: licensed financial institutions regulated by NRB, the Ministry of Finance, and authorised domestic entities that provide payment and clearing services. Licensed Financial Institutions must apply to participate using the forms provided.~

column2: ~Who regulates the licensed financial institutions eligible for RTGS membership?~
column3: ~Licensed financial institutions are regulated by NRB (Nepal Rastra Bank).~

column2: ~How do licensed financial institutions apply to participate in the RTGS system?~
column3: ~Licensed Financial Institutions must apply to participate using the forms provided (See Appendix 1).~

column2: ~How many modes of participation are there in the RTGS system?~
column3: ~There are three modes of participation in the RTGS system.~

column2: ~Who is a Direct Participant in the RTGS system?~
column3: ~A Direct Participant has a settlement account in the RTGS system and has an appropriate communication platform to monitor and manage their account position directly from the RTGS system. A Direct Participant has the technical ability to send and receive payment orders and other messages to/from the RTGS system.

column2: ~What technical ability does a Direct Participant have?~
column3: ~A Direct Participant has the technical ability to send and receive payment orders and other messages to/from the RTGS system.~

column2: ~Who is an Indirect Participant in the RTGS system?~
column3: ~An Indirect Participant has a settlement account in the RTGS system and may or may not have an appropriate communication platform to monitor and manage their account position directly from the RTGS system. They shall appoint another Direct Participant to send and/or receive payment orders and other messages to/from the RTGS system on their behalf.~

column2: ~Can an Indirect Participant manage its account position directly?~
column3: ~An Indirect Participant may or may not have an appropriate communication platform to monitor and manage their account position directly from the RTGS system. They can appoint a Direct Participant to send and/or receive payment orders and other messages to/from the RTGS system on their behalf.~

column2: ~What is a Special Member in the RTGS system?~
column3: ~A Special Member is like a clearing house, securities settlement system, or any organization recognized by NRB under this category. They may directly connect to the RTGS System for submission of DNS files. These clearing entities that have been granted membership or limited access to the RTGS System have to abide by the terms and conditions stipulated in the MOU with NRB and this System Rules.~

column2: ~What must clearing entities with limited access to the RTGS System do?~
column3: ~The clearing entities that have been granted membership or limited access to the RTGS System have to abide by the terms and conditions stipulated in the MOU with NRB and this System Rules.~

column2: ~What must an institution do to become an Indirect Participant?~
column3: ~An Institution planning to be an Indirect Participant must apply for appointment of its Settlement Agent Bank using the forms provided (See Appendix 2).~

column2: ~What are the key responsibilities of the Service Provider in managing the RTGS system?~
column3: ~The Service Provider must:

column1: *   Operate the RTGS system for all members in a safe and efficient manner.
*   Publish user manuals and documentation of the RTGS system.
*   Maintain the daily operations of the RTGS system for all members.
*   Establish a Help Desk for all participants.
*   Reschedule business day sessions if necessary.
*   Manage participant status (perform suspension and termination if required).
*   Issue security tokens for Users and manage User statuses in an emergency.
*   Provide archived data to participants as requested.
*   Provide a Help Desk contact number for Participants to contact with functional, operational or technical problems.
*   Consult with the system supplier for the fast resolution of high impact problems.~

column2: ~How many Direct Participants can an Indirect Participant appoint as their intermediary agent?~
column3: ~An Indirect Participant can appoint only one Direct Participant as their intermediary agent.~

column2: ~How should an Indirect Participant notify the Service Provider about the appointed Direct Participant?~
column3: ~An Indirect Participant should notify the identity of the Direct Participant to the Service Provider in writing at least three (3) months before such service comes into effect in the RTGS system, or as prescribed by NRB.~

column2: ~What documents must an Indirect Participant submit for appointing an intermediary agent?~
column3: ~An Indirect Participant shall submit the following documents:

column1: *   Completed application letter for Participation in the RTGS system
*   Completed application letter as Indirect Participant for the appointment of intermediary agent
*   Service Agreement with only one Direct Participant.~

column2: ~What functions can the appointed Direct Participant perform for an Indirect Participant?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, the appointed Direct Participant can perform the following functions for an Indirect Participant:

column1: *   Send payment orders and other messages to the RTGS system.
*   Receive payment orders and other messages from the RTGS system.
*   Debit Indirect Participant settlement account.
*   View account information.
*   Establish and operate the technical connection for these functions.~

column2: ~How should changes to the appointed intermediary agent or its functions be communicated?~
column3: ~Indirect Participant shall submit any application in writing regarding any changes to the appointed intermediary agent or its authorised functions or services to the Service Provider at least one calendar (1) month before such changes come into effect in the RTGS system.~

column2: ~Who must an RTGS Participant appoint to provide net settlement files?~
column3: ~An RTGS Participant shall appoint a Clearing House to provide their net settlement files to be settled through RTGS.~

column2: ~What type of system is a Clearing House?~
column3: ~A Clearing House is a Deferred Net Settlement System.~

column2: ~What must each Clearing House sign with NRB?~
column3: ~Each Clearing House must sign a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with the NRB, agreeing to be bound by, and conform to, the requirements of these system rules.~

column2: ~What does the Clearing House agree to by signing the MOU?~
column3: ~The Clearing House agrees to be bound by, and conform to, the requirements of these system rules by signing a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with the NRB.~

column2: ~What documents does the Service Provider publish for participants?~
column3: ~The Service Provider must publish user manuals and documentation of the RTGS system required to the participants for operation in the RTGS system.~

column2: ~What daily task does the Service Provider maintain for members?~
column3: ~The Service Provider must maintain the daily operations of the RTGS system for all members.~

column2: ~What support facility does the Service Provider establish for participants?~
column3: ~The Service Provider establishes a Help Desk for all participants to provide RTGS services.~

column2: ~How does the Service Provider manage participant status?~
column3: ~The Service Provider manages participant status by performing suspension and termination if required (6.6).~

column2: ~What does the Service Provider issue to users in the system?~
column3: ~The Service Provider issues security tokens for Users and manages User statuses in an emergency.~

column2: ~What data does the Service Provider provide to participants upon request?~
column3: ~The Service Provider provides archived data to participants as requested.

column2: ~What must a Participant ensure before sending a payment to the RTGS system?~
column3: ~The Participant shall ensure that all the required payment information is correct before sending the payment to the RTGS system.~

column2: ~Who is liable for the payments made by a Participant?~
column3: ~The Participant shall be liable as principal in respect of its payments.~

column2: ~What should a Participant do if there is a problem affecting its participation in RTGS?~
column3: ~The Participant shall advise Service Provider of any problem which might affect its participation in the RTGS (environment contingency issues, disconnections, etc.). Additionally, if a Participant has lost its connection to the RTGS system, it may use the Service Provider contingency facilities. If there is a technical failure or disruption affecting its connection, the Participant shall notify the Service Provider immediately and update them every thirty (30) minutes on their recovery status and action plan until they resume normal operations.

column2: ~What role does a Direct Participant have regarding Indirect Participants?~
column3: ~A Direct Participant acts as an intermediary agent to ensure delivery of payments from and to Indirect Participants, if appointed for it. Additionally, an Indirect Participant may appoint only one Direct Participant as their intermediary agent for sending and/or receiving payment orders and other messages to/from the RTGS system.~

column2: ~What must each Participant ensure about their Settlement Account for transactions?~
column3: ~Each Participant shall ensure that it is able to provide sufficient funds in its Settlement Account to ensure settlement of each transaction on value date and to ensure immediate settlement of Net Clearing Position from NCHL or other Clearing House and of DOMS and in due course CSD payments.~

column2: ~What reserves must Participants maintain in their Settlement Account?~
column3: ~Participants shall maintain sufficient balances in their RTGS settlement account to meet its payment and settlement obligations in a timely manner. They must also ensure they have sufficient reserves to ensure immediate settlement of Net Clearing Position from NCHL or other Clearing House and of DOMS and in due course CSD payments.

column2: ~What must Participants do regarding connectivity to the RTGS System?~
column3: ~Each Participant should operate, administer and monitor their own connectivity to the RTGS System and to develop their internal systems for linking to / from the RTGS System and ensure the maintenance, security and reliability (including back-up and contingency arrangements) of their own systems at their own cost. Direct Participants must install, maintain and operate a virtual private network connection, with backup, while Indirect Participants must also install, maintain and operate a virtual private network connection, with backup.~

column2: ~Who is responsible for maintaining, securing, and backing up internal systems?~
column3: ~The document states that the Board and higher management should oversee the functioning of IT operations and ensure a safe IT operation environment. It also emphasizes the need for segregation of duty and documented standards/procedures for administering application systems. Critical system functions, including backups and security measures, should be carried out in joint custody.~

column2: ~What must Participants do to introduce new Users to the RTGS system?~
column3: ~Participants must apply to NRB for new User security tokens and introduce Users to the system (see form in Appendix 8).~

column2: ~How are Deferred Net Settlement system clearing balances settled?~
column3: ~Deferred Net Settlement system clearing balances are settled through RTGS. Each Clearing House must sign a Memorandum of Understanding with the NRB in which they agree to be bound by, and conform to, the requirements of these system rules.~

column2: ~How are government or NRB securities transactions paid?~
column3: ~Payment of the cash leg of government/NRB securities transactions is done through DvP mechanisms.~

column2: ~What types of payments are exchanged with NRB?~
column3: ~Payments exchanged with NRB include payments of amounts due to NRB, monetary policy operations, liquidity facilities, cash withdrawals and deposits, or other types where NRB is an intermediary.~

column2: ~Can NRB set minimum or maximum payment amounts in the RTGS system?~
column3: ~NRB may set a minimum payment amount in the RTGS system, which may be varied from time to time and is shown in Appendix 7. There is no maximum value limit to an RTGS transaction.~

column2: ~How must a Participant submit messages in the RTGS system?~
column3: ~According to the provided text, all Direct Participants must submit their transactions to RTGS using Straight-Through Processing (STP) methods. Messages will be submitted in real-time once they have been entered in the Core Banking System (or other payment origination system) and checked against client funds availability. The Participant shall submit messages according to the specification for the RTGS system as set out in the Message Specifications.

column2: ~What types of messages does the RTGS system support?~
column3: ~The RTGS system supports a series of payment and non-payment messages for submission of payment orders, queries, queue management, and text messages in SWIFT MX (ISO 20022) format and receiving of payments and notifications. It also supports messages like admi.002 (rejection of a message due to format errors) and admi.004 (notification of an event in the central system).

column2: ~In what format are RTGS messages submitted and received?~
column3: ~RTGS messages are submitted and received in SWIFT MX (ISO 20022) format.~

column2: ~What notifications can participants receive through the RTGS system?~
column3: ~Participants can receive acknowledgements of received messages and error reports in real-time. They also receive payment and notification messages, statements of their account positions and transactions at the end of the business day, and response messages providing information about the position of their settlement account, reserves, the status of a given payment, reserved amounts, system and business day information.

column2: ~What does the RTGS system validate regarding the issuer of a message?~
column3: ~The RTGS system validates: i) Identification and authentication of the issuer (Participant, and specific user); and ii) Access rights of the participant and the user.~

column2: ~When do settlements occur in the RTGS system?~
column3: ~Settlements in the RTGS system occur following the reception and validation of a transfer order received from a participant, based on the following principles: technical acceptance into the RTGS system, processing of payments according to priority, immediate acceptance for settlement if funds are sufficient (otherwise, the transfer is queued), and simultaneous debiting and crediting of accounts. Additionally, settlement occurs only if the system is running, the business day is open, the settlement process isn’t suspended, accounts aren’t locked, the debiting participant has sufficient funds or a reservation, the issuer has access rights, and there's no higher-priority queued transaction.

column2: ~What happens if a transfer has technical errors during validation?~
column3: ~The system will reject and return the transfer to the issuer in case of technical errors.~

column2: ~How are payments processed in the RTGS system?~
column3: ~To transact through the RTGS system, one must go to the bank or financial institution where one has an account and submit the details of the payment order in the prescribed format. Once the payment order received in this way is entered into the RTGS system by the bank or financial institution, the transaction is completed immediately. Payments are processed according to their priority and order of acceptance by the RTGS system, and are deemed accepted for settlement and thus irrevocable when they have passed the test for insufficient funds and are settled immediately or from the queue.

column2: end
~When is a payment deemed accepted and irrevocable?~
column3: ~A payment is deemed accepted for settlement and is thus irrevocable when it has passed the test for insufficient funds and is thus immediately settled or settled from the queue.~

column2: ~What conditions must be met for settlement to occur?~
column3: ~For settlement to occur in the RTGS system, the following conditions must be met:

column1: 1.  The system has started and the business day has opened.
2.  The settlement process is not suspended.
3.  The debiting account is not locked for debit and the crediting account is not locked for credit.
4.  The debiting Participant's status is valid.
5.  The debiting Participant is not suspended for debits.
6.  The debiting Participant has sufficient funds in their settlement account to ensure settlement of each transaction on the value date.~

column2: ~How quickly must beneficiary participants credit a received RTGS transaction?~
column3: ~Beneficiary Participants have an obligation to credit the beneficiary customer of a received RTGS transaction within a reasonable time. The SERVICE PROVIDER expects a payment to be credited in real time by Straight Through Processing (STP) and within 30 minutes of receipt for the exceptional situation of manual posting.~

column2: ~What is the maximum forward value date a Participant can submit for payment orders?~
column3: ~The Participant may submit payment orders with forward value dates not exceeding ten (10) business days.~

column2: ~What happens to valid payment orders with a forward value date?~
column3: ~Valid payment orders with a forward value date are settled on their value date.~

column2: ~What network must all Participants and the Service Provider use to connect to the RTGS system?~
column3: ~All Participants and the Service Provider must use a virtual private network.~

column2: ~What functions must a Direct Participant’s VPN support?~
column3: ~A Direct Participant’s VPN must support the following functions: Message processing, Queue management, Account management, Online enquiry, and Reports.~

column2: ~What functions must an Indirect Participant’s VPN support?~
column3: ~An Indirect Participant’s virtual private network (VPN) connection must allow them to perform the following functions in the RTGS system: Online enquiry and Reports.~

column2: ~What security measures must Participating Banks comply with?~
column3: ~Participating Banks shall comply with security measures (including related instructions, rules and requirements) specified by the Service Provider particularly on the following: connecting to the CMA modules that mediate the interface between Participants and the RTGS - either the Straight-Through Processing Adapter (STPA) or Straight Through Processing Gateway (STPG), or other suitable software acquired by the Participant; connectivity platform for sending and receiving messages in the RTGS; and safeguarding and managing their public and private keys as well as their associated PKI key certificates used for RTGS communication to ensure such information will not be compromised by unauthorised persons.~

column2: ~How should Banks connect to the RTGS system?~
column3: ~Banks should connect to the RTGS system by connecting to the CMA modules that mediate the interface between Participants and the RTGS - either the Straight-Through Processing Adapter (STPA) or Straight Through Processing Gateway (STPG), or other suitable software acquired by the Participant; and by using a connectivity platform for sending and receiving messages in the RTGS system.~

column2: ~What PKI measures must Participating Banks adopt?~
column3: ~Participating Banks must comply with security measures specified by the Service Provider, including: connecting to the CMA modules; using a connectivity platform for sending and receiving messages; and safeguarding and managing their public and private keys as well as their associated PKI key certificates used for RTGS communication to ensure such information will not be compromised by unauthorized persons.~

column2: ~How should Banks manage their public and private keys?~
column3: ~Participating Banks shall comply with security measures, particularly on safeguarding and managing their public and private keys as well as their associated PKI key certificates used for RTGS communication to ensure such information will not be compromised by unauthorised persons. Keys shall be created, stored, distributed, or changed under the most stringent conditions and have the frequency of key changes depending on the sensitivity of data and operational criticality.~

column2: ~What must a Participant do if their authentication is compromised or lost?~
column3: ~A Participant whose authentication has been compromised, lost, revoked or unfit for use shall immediately notify the Service Provider in writing.~

column2: ~What happens to pending payment orders if a Participant’s authentication is compromised?~
column3: ~All payment orders or instructions pending in the RTGS system by that Participant shall be rejected, but any such payment orders or instruction which has been settled by the RTGS system shall be and remain irrevocable in the RTGS system.~

column2: ~What is the purpose of the Start of Business Day period in RTGS?~
column3: ~The purpose of the Start of Business Day period in RTGS is for initialization of the business day, parameter setting, and data processing.~

column2: ~What activities occur during the Account Housekeeping period?~
column3: ~Account management: Debit of amounts due to NRB, limits set up for Collateral Accounts by Service Provider, if required by Participant (until the CSD automated procedure can be used).~

column2: ~What can Participants do during the Morning Exchange period?~
column3: ~During the Morning Exchange period, Participants can invoke the RTGS transfers for today sent in previous days, and RTGS also invokes payments that have been announced for that date. They can exchange interbank transfers and customer transfers, and request an ILF if temporarily short of liquidity. Settlement of transactions from external systems can also take place during this period.

column2: ~How does the Afternoon Exchange period differ from the Morning Exchange period?~
column3: ~The Afternoon Exchange period has a different fee than the Morning Exchange period. The fee for the Afternoon Exchange period is Rs. 20, whereas the Morning Exchange period fee is Rs. 10 for Customer and Interbank Payments. Additionally, during the Morning period, a Participant can exchange interbank transfers and customer transfers, and can request an ILF if temporarily short of liquidity. The Afternoon period is described as "Same as Morning Exchange period but with different fee."~

column2: ~What can Participants do during the Morning Exchange period?~
column3: ~During the Morning Exchange period, Participants can invoke the RTGS transfers for today sent in previous days, RTGS also invokes payments that have been announced for that date. They can exchange interbank transfers and customer transfers, and can also request an ILF if temporarily short of liquidity. Settlement of transactions from external systems can also take place.

column2: ~What occurs during the Reports Generation period RTGS?~
column3: ~The RTGS system generates messages with account balances and statement reports and sends them to Participants for reconciliation. Billing Reports are also generated for participants according to the billing cycle. Billing rates are stated in these Rules in Appendix 5.

column2: ~What account types are available in the RTGS system?~
column3: ~The following account types are available in the RTGS system: Settlement Account and Collateral Management Account.~

column2: ~Which currencies can a Participant open accounts in?~
column3: ~A Participant can open accounts in the following currencies: Nepalese Rupee (NPR), US Dollar (USD), Euro (EUR), Pound Sterling (GBP), and Japanese Yen (JPY).~

column2: ~What IT-related issues have emerged in the banking sector?~
column3: ~The use of information technology by financial institutions has created challenges including managing and governing IT. Specific IT-related issues that have emerged in the banking sector are: tackling changes in technology, migrating systems, maintaining adequate internal control, limiting unauthorized access to systems and data, securing electronic transactions, meeting legal requirements, managing outsourcing services, mitigating IT-related risks, and addressing cyber fraud. New delivery channels like ATMs, internet banking, and mobile banking have increased the risk of financial loss and electronic fraud.~

column2: ~How have new delivery channels affected banking risks?~
column3: ~New delivery channels such as ATM, internet banking, and mobile banking have increased the risk of financial loss and electronic frauds along with other banking risks.~

column2: ~Why has NRB decided to regulate IT activities in commercial banks?~
column3: ~NRB has felt necessary to regulate and guide IT related activities in commercial banks with the objectives of strengthening banks for tackling with emerging cyber frauds, managing information technology prudently and mitigating risk aroused from implementation of information technology.~

column2: ~Why is IT governance essential in commercial banks?~
column3: ~IT governance is essential in commercial banks because IT is very critical in supporting and enabling business goals and is strategic for business growth. Since IT has been widely adopted by commercial banks, it has increased the challenge on governing it.~

column2: ~What should banks have regarding IT strategy and policy?~
column3: ~Bank should have a board approved IT related strategy and policy and IT policy should be reviewed at least annually. IT strategy can be long term and short term and long term strategy should be mapped to short term strategy periodically.~

column2: ~How should the IT organizational structure be designed?~
column3: ~The IT organizational structure for banks should be commensurate with the size, scale, and nature of business activities carried out by the bank and may differ from bank to bank. Broadly, the organization structure consists of Development, Technology, IT Operation, and Information Assurance.

column2: ~What should banks assess regarding IT personnel?~
column3: ~Banks should assess the requirement of expertise to successfully complete required IT functions. A periodic IT training requirement for IT personnel according to the IT functions of the bank should be assessed.~

column2: ~Why is robust information security crucial for banks in achieving business goals and managing risk prudently?~
column3: ~Robust information security is crucial for banks to achieve business goals and for managing risk prudently because it ensures accuracy, integrity, consistency, completeness, validity, timeliness, accessibility, usability, and auditability of information.~

column2: ~What should a board-approved Information Security Policy cover in banks?~
column3: ~The board-approved Information Security Policy should address all electronic delivery channels and payment systems.~

column2: ~How often should banks conduct risk assessment for their information assets?~
column3: ~Banks should conduct Risk Assessment periodically (at least annually) for each asset that has possibility of impacting the CIA of the information of the bank.

column2: ~How should banks ensure employees, consultants, and contractors comply with information security policies?~
column3: ~Banks can ensure compliance by:

column1: 1.  Communicating the board-approved Information Security Policy to employees, contractors/suppliers, consultants, and officials.
2.  Ensuring awareness and compliance through clear job descriptions, employee agreements, policy awareness, and policy acknowledgements.
3.  Conducting information security awareness programs periodically for employees, vendors, and other related stakeholders, customizing the programs according to the target group.~

column2: ~What physical and environmental controls should banks implement?~
column3: ~Banks should implement appropriate physical and environmental controls taking into consideration of threats, and based on the entity’s unique geographical location, building configuration, neighboring entities etc to secure critical hardware, system and information. Physical access to datacenter and critical equipment rooms must be restricted to authorized individuals only.~

column2: ~Why is information security considered an ongoing process in banks?~
column3: ~Information security is not a one-time activity and cannot be gained by just purchasing and installing suitable hardware or software. Banks should institutionalize processes to regularly assess the security health of the organization and detect and fix vulnerabilities. It is recommended to conduct penetration testing of the system periodically.~

column2: ~How should banks configure and maintain their systems for security?~
column3: ~Banks should harden their systems by configuring them with the highest level of security settings in the operating system, firewall, and system software. The default password should be changed immediately after installation, and updates, patches, and security enhancements should be installed as recommended by vendors. Banks should also implement a change management process for handling changes, develop and implement comprehensive computer virus protection mechanisms, and deploy strong cryptography and end-to-end encryption to protect sensitive data.

column2: ~What measures should banks take to protect sensitive customer data?~
column3: ~Banks should implement several measures to protect sensitive customer data, including:

column1: *   Deploying strong cryptography and end-to-end encryption to protect customer PINs, user passwords, and other sensitive data in networks and in storage.
*   Installing firewalls between internal and external networks as well as between geographically separate sites, configured according to the bank's network security policy.
*   Encrypting transaction information and PIN/Password from mobile devices to the bank's system when providing banking services using mobile devices.
*   Replacing current magnetic stripe cards with chip-based cards.
*   Authenticating online payments using a second factor and providing instant alerts to customers via email/SMS/automated voice call.
*   Implementing additional controls like daily and per-transaction limits for mobile banking.~

column2: ~What precautions should banks follow for ATM and card transactions?~
column3: ~Banks should employ secure transmission of messages using appropriate encryption from ATMs, controls relating to ATM key generation, loading, and destruction. They should also use firewalls, antivirus measures, secure PIN generation, and adequate segregation of duty while creating PINs and cards. It is recommended to replace existing signature-based systems with PIN-based authorization systems at POS terminals and withdraw non-PIN based terminals. Banks should also replace current magnetic stripe cards with chip-based cards and authenticate online card payments using a second factor, providing instant alerts to customers via email/SMS/automated voice call. Additionally, they should ensure that electronic cards and their PINs are not under the control of a single person from production till delivery to the customer.

column2: ~How should mobile banking security be managed by banks?~
column3: ~Banks should provide banking services using mobile devices with the following security measures: information stored on mobile devices should be encrypted, transaction information and PIN/Password should be encrypted from mobile devices to the bank's system, and daily/per-transaction limits should be defined for fund transfers. Mobile banking should be allowed for accounts in Nepalese currency only.~

column2: ~What is recommended for web and internet banking security?~
column3: ~Banks are recommended to implement more than one factor for authenticating critical activities like fund transfers through internet banking facility. The authentication methodology should commensurate with the risk of internet banking. Bank should implement adequate security measure to secure their web applications from traditional and emerging cyber threats and attacks and critical application should employ latest SSL encryption.~

column2: ~Why has the introduction of electronic delivery channels intensified challenges in banking?~
column3: ~The introduction of electronic delivery channels has intensified challenges in banking because customers no longer need to visit bank branches physically to conduct banking. This has intensified the challenges of authenticating customers, and fraudsters are designing and using more advanced techniques to impersonate users and make illegal access to customer accounts. Additionally, new delivery channels such as ATM, internet banking, and mobile banking have increased the risk of financial loss and electronic frauds along with other banking risks.~

column2: ~Who should banks educate to create effective information security practices?~
column3: ~Banks should educate other stakeholders including its employees, vendors, customers, and related stakeholders to create effective information security practice.

column2: ~What should an information security awareness program include according to the guidelines?~
column3: ~According to the guidelines, an information security awareness program should be developed and periodically conducted to employees, vendors, customers, and other related stakeholders. The program should be customized according to the target group, and mechanisms should be developed to track the effectiveness of the training program.

column2: ~How should banks ensure customers protect their devices and systems when using electronic banking?~
column3: ~Bank should ensure that customers are adequately educated so that they take appropriate security measures to protect their devices and computer systems and ensure that their hardware or system integrity is not compromised when engaging in electronic banking. Bank should have appropriate procedures in place to promptly respond to customer queries regarding securely accessing electronic banking.~

column2: ~What is the role of banks in authenticating customers for electronic banking?~
column3: ~Banks are responsible for the safety and soundness of their systems. They should use appropriate customer authentication systems to authenticate customers before access to the system is allowed and ensure customers are adequately educated and aware of securing their credentials. Furthermore, banks should implement more than one factor for authenticating critical activities like fund transfers through internet banking facility, with the authentication methodology commensurate with the risk of internet banking.~

column2: ~What type of information should banks provide to customers about electronic banking?~
column3: ~Banks should clearly provide information about the services, cost, security features, risk, and benefits of the electronic banking environment. They should also provide precise information about the responsibilities, obligations, and rights of customers and the bank regarding electronic transactions. Additionally, banks should publish customer privacy and security policy and the cost of transaction.

column2: ~How should banks explain the resolution process for security breaches or fraudulent access to accounts?~
column3: ~Banks should publish clear information about the dispute or problem resolution process in case of any security breaches and fraudulent access to customer's account. The condition on which loss will be attributable to the bank or their customers should be clearly explained.~

column2: ~Where should banks publish customer privacy, security policies, and transaction costs?~
column3: ~Banks should publish customer privacy and security policy; cost of transaction etc. in their website or at the time of subscription of the corresponding electronic delivery channels.~

column2: ~How should banks inform customers about transaction costs at ATMs and other electronic channels?~
column3: ~Banks should clearly inform users on the amount transaction cost at each of their ATM location or electronically before committing the transactions from ATM and other electronic delivery channels.

column2: ~What mechanism should banks have for handling disputes and grievances in electronic banking?~
column3: ~Banks should develop a dispute handling mechanism with expected timing of bank response to handle disputed payments, transactions, and other issues in electronic banking delivery channels. They should also formulate a procedure for handling grievances and publish clear information about the dispute or problem resolution process in case of any security breaches and fraudulent access to customer's account, explaining the conditions on which loss will be attributable to the bank or their customers. Additionally, the bank shall be responsible for grievance handling in case of customer complaints.

column2: ~How should banks educate customers about the risks and protections in e-banking services?~
column3: ~Banks should provide clear information to their customers about the risks and benefits of using e-banking delivery services to enable customers to decide on choosing such services. They should also educate customers on which protections are provided and not provided in each of their delivery channels.

column2: ~What responsibilities do banks have when outsourcing IT functions such as inter-branch communication, software, hardware, or emerging technologies?~
column3: ~Banks have several responsibilities when outsourcing IT functions:

column1: *   **Due diligence, oversight, and risk management:** Board and senior management are responsible for due diligence, oversight, and management of risks associated with outsourcing, with accountability resting with the board and bank management.

*   **Risk evaluation:** Banks should evaluate the risk before entering into an outsourcing agreement for technical operations that can significantly impact the business operation and reputation of the bank, and this evaluation should be periodic.

*   **Information security and privacy:** All outsourced operations should adhere to the bank's information security and privacy policy. Banks must ensure the outsource service provider implements adequate internal controls, logical access controls, and physical security controls.

*   **Regulatory interference:** Banks must ensure that outsourcing of IT operations does not interfere or obstruct regulatory activities. Agreements should recognize the authority of regulatory bodies to inspect service providers.

*   **Monitoring and control:** Banks should establish a process for monitoring and controlling outsourcing activities commensurate with the nature, scope, complexity, and inherent risk of the outsourced activity. Accountability for performance should be specified in service level agreements and evaluated periodically.
*   **Data protection:** Banks are responsible for ensuring data availability, integrity, and confidentiality, even if the service provider outsources some of its functions.

*   **Service quality:** Banks should ensure that the outsourcing arrangement does not adversely affect the availability and quality of banking services. Contingency plans should address alternate service providers or bringing functions back in-house.~

column2: ~Who is responsible for overseeing risks associated with outsourcing IT functions in banks?~
column3: ~Board and senior management are responsible for due diligence, oversight, and management of risks associated with outsourcing and accountability of outsourcing decision rests with board and bank management.~

column2: ~What should banks do before entering into an outsourcing agreement for technical operations?~
column3: ~Banks should evaluate the risk before entering into an outsourcing agreement of technical operations that can significantly impact the business operation and reputation of the bank and it should be evaluated periodically.~

column2: ~How should banks ensure outsourced operations comply with information security and privacy requirements?~
column3: ~Banks should ensure that all outsourced operations are subject to their information security and privacy policy and ensure that the outsource service provider implements adequate internal controls, logical access control, and physical security controls to ensure the same.~

column2: ~How should outsourcing agreements address regulatory oversight?~
column3: ~Outsourcing agreements must recognize the authority of regulatory bodies under the BAFIA and NRB Act to inspect, supervise, or examine the service provider's role, responsibilities, obligations, functions, systems, and facilities.~

column2: ~What processes should banks establish to monitor and control outsourcing activities?~
column3: ~Banks should establish a process for monitoring and control of outsourcing activities that is commensurate with the nature, scope, complexity, and inherent risk of the outsourced activity. Furthermore, the accountability of performance of outsourced activities should be specified in the agreed service level and it should be evaluated periodically. A periodic review of operational and financial condition of the outsource service provider should also be carried out.~

column2: ~How should banks ensure availability, integrity, and confidentiality of services when functions are outsourced?~
column3: ~Banks should ensure availability, integrity, and confidentiality requirements even if outsourced service provider is outsourcing some or all of its functions. All outsourced operations should be subject to the bank's information security and privacy policy, and the bank should ensure that the outsource service provider implements adequate internal controls, logical access control, and physical security controls to ensure the same.~

column2: ~What considerations should banks take when outsourcing IT operations abroad?~
column3: ~When outsourcing IT operations abroad, banks should consider country risk factors such as economic, technology and processes. They should also clarify the jurisdiction for their data and applicable regulations at the beginning of the arrangement, reviewing this periodically and addressing significant changes made by the service provider. Social and political reasons should also be considered while assessing the risk of outsourcing, and the bank should proactively evaluate such risks and develop appropriate mitigating controls and, if required, an exit strategy. Moreover, the authority of regulatory bodies to inspect the service provider's role should be recognized in agreements.~

column2: ~How can banks ensure continuity of critical applications when outsourcing?~
column3: ~Bank can either receive source code and its updates from the vendor or can arrange a software escrow agreement to ensure source code and its updates availability in case the vendor goes out of business.~

column2: ~What should banks clarify regarding data jurisdiction and regulations when using emerging technologies like virtualization or cloud computing?~
column3: ~Banks should clarify the jurisdiction for their data and applicable regulations at the beginning of an outsourcing or offshoring arrangement, and this information should be reviewed periodically, especially if significant changes are performed by the service provider.~

column2: ~What role should the board and higher management play in overseeing IT operations in banks?~
column3: ~The board and higher management should oversee the functioning of IT operations and ensure a safe IT operation environment.~

column2: ~How should banks enforce segregation of duties and access controls in IT operations?~
column3: ~Banks should enforce segregation of duty in all IT operations and implement access controls based on the principle of “need to know” and 'least privilege'. Additionally, there should be documented standards/procedures for administering an application system, approved by the application owner and kept up-to-date. Critical system functions should be carried out in joint custody.~

column2: ~Which critical system functions should be carried out in joint custody in banks’ IT operations?~
column3: ~Critical system functions such as systems initialization, network security configuration, access control system installation, changing operating system parameters, implementing firewalls and intrusion prevention systems, modifying contingency plans, invoking emergency procedures, obtaining access to backup recovery resources, administering critical application, creating master password and cryptographic keys should be carried out in joint custody.

column2: ~What is the purpose of a “change management” process in IT operations?~
column3: ~The purpose of a “change management” process is to ensure that changes to technology and processes are recorded, assessed, authorized, prioritized, planned, tested, implemented, documented, and reviewed in a controlled manner and environment.~

column2: ~What should a bank’s migration policy include to ensure secure and reliable data transfer?~
column3: ~A bank's migration policy should include details of the migration process to ensure the principle of information security. It also needs to include explicit sign-offs from application owners after each stage and upon completion of the migration, to ensure data integrity, completeness, and consistency. Additionally, the policy needs to ensure an audit trail of the older system is available even after migration to the new system, including pre-migration, migration, and post-migration reviews.

column2: ~How should vendors, suppliers, or consultants accessing critical systems be managed?~
column3: ~Vendors, suppliers or consultant who are authorized to access critical system of the bank should be subject to close supervision, monitoring and access control similar to those applying to internal staffs.~

column2: ~What measures should banks take to ensure high availability of IT services in an online environment?~
column3: ~Bank should ensure that they have adequate resources in terms of hardware, software and other operating capability to deliver consistently reliable service. Bank should identify and maintain standby software, hardware and network components critical for availability. The datacenter, disaster recovery solution, enterprise network and security and branch or delivery channels should be designed and configured for high availability and no single point of failure.~

column2: ~Why should banks conduct periodic risk assessments of their IT environment?~
column3: ~Banks should conduct periodic risk assessments of their IT environment to identify probable events or activities that can adversely affect the bank's operation or reputation and to ensure suitable mitigating strategies are in place.~

column2: ~What should be documented and approved before developing software that handles financial information in banks?~
column3: ~User functional requirements, security requirements, performance requirements, and technical specifications should be documented and approved by the appropriate level of management before software is developed.~

column2: ~How should information security requirements be incorporated during software development?~
column3: ~Information security requirements should be incorporated at each stage of the software development lifecycle. Security requirements relating to access control, authentication, transaction authorization, system activity logging, audit trail, data integrity, security event tracking, etc., should be incorporated along with business requirements.~

column2: ~What is required of an audit trail in banking applications?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, the following is required of an audit trail in banking applications:

column1: *   It should be detailed enough to be used as forensic evidence and meet regulatory and legal requirements.
*   It should be detailed enough to comply with regulatory, legal, and bank requirements.
*   It should be secured to ensure the integrity of information.
*   It should be available even after migration of the system, if applicable, including pre-migration, migration, and post-migration audit trails.
*   It should include transaction id, date, time, originator id, authorizer id, and action taken.~

column2: ~Why are banks encouraged to conduct a source code review of applications?~
column3: ~Banks are encouraged to conduct source code review of applications with the objective of finding loopholes and defects residing in the software due to poor programming practice, coding errors, or malicious attempts. All vulnerabilities, loopholes, and defects found should be fixed before system implementation.~

column2: ~What is the purpose of Business Continuity Planning (BCP) in banks and what key aspects should it include?~
column3: ~The purpose of Business Continuity Planning (BCP) in banks is to minimize financial, operational, legal, reputational, and other risks while ensuring the continuity, resumption, and recovery of business processes.

column1: A BCP should generally incorporate:
*   Business Impact analysis
*   Recovery strategies
*   Testing, training, awareness, communication, and crisis management program.
*   Allocation of sufficient resources and knowledgeable personnel.
*   Consideration of all probable natural and man-made disasters, security threats, regulatory requirements, dependencies on outsourcing activities, and issues of operating in multiple countries.
*   Specification of Recovery Point Objective (RPO) and Recovery Time Objective (RTO) of business processes.~

column2: ~Who should be responsible for developing and maintaining the Business Continuity Plan in a bank?~
column3: ~A senior officer of the bank should be appointed as Head of Business Continuity Planning function and he/she will be responsible for developing BCP, its regular updates, prioritization of critical business activities, recovery, testing, training and other aspects of BCP.~

column2: ~What factors should a BCP consider when being developed for a bank?~
column3: ~A BCP should consider all probable natural and man-made disasters, security threats, regulatory requirements, dependencies on outsourcing activities, and issues of operating in multiple countries. It should also consider the people aspect along with the technical aspects.~

column2: ~How should a bank ensure that its BCP is effective?~
column3: ~BCP should be periodically, at least annually, tested to ensure its effectiveness. The testing should include all aspects and constituents of the bank i.e. people, processes and resources including technology. BCP testing should be both planned and unplanned and should be audited by internal audit of the bank. The testing and its outcome should be documented and amendments in BCP be made as suggested by the outcome of the test.

column2: ~What are RPO and RTO in the context of BCP, and how should a bank meet them?~
column3: ~RPO (Recovery Point Objective) and RTO (Recovery Time Objective) are specified in the BCP for business processes. To meet these requirements, a bank can choose a Hot Site, Warm Site, or Cold Site for backup, but the chosen site must meet the specified RPO and RTO. Banks can also use their own standby site or outsource it, potentially opting for high availability systems, data replication, or backups to offsite locations, or a combination of these strategies.

column2: ~What strategies can banks use for disaster recovery site arrangements?~
column3: ~Banks can use their own standby site and system or outsource it from disaster recovery providers. Depending on RPO and RTO requirements, banks may opt for high availability systems to keep both system and data replicated on a remote site or live replication of data to an offsite location, backups made to offsite location or backups made to electronic media and sent offsite periodically, or a combination of these strategies. They can also choose from Hot Sites, Warm Sites, or Cold Sites for backup sites.~

column2: ~What disaster mitigating measures should banks adopt for their datacenter and critical systems?~
column3: ~Banks should adopt disaster mitigating strategies such as locally mirroring data and system, arranging UPS and generator for long term power failure, using surge protector to minimize the effect of power fluctuations, and providing adequate physical and environmental controls in the DC. The datacenter, disaster recovery solution, enterprise network, security and branch or delivery channels should be designed and configured for high availability and no single point of failure. The location of the building containing the datacenter and critical equipment rooms must be chosen to minimize the risk of natural and man-made disasters. Physical access to the datacenter and critical equipment rooms must be restricted to authorized individuals only.

column2: ~How should banks select the location of their datacenter and critical equipment rooms?~
column3: ~The location of buildings containing datacenter and critical equipment rooms must be chosen so as to minimize the risk of natural and man-made disasters, flood, fire, explosion, riots, and environmental hazards.~

column2: ~How can banks ensure integrity between the datacenter and disaster recovery site?~
column3: ~Bank should check transaction and data integrity between DC and DR site periodically. It is recommended to make this check as a part of End of Day (EOD) or Beginning of Day (BOD) process.~

column2: ~What should be included in a bank’s incident response plan for business continuity?~
column3: ~A bank’s incident response plan for business continuity should, inter alia, cover:

column1: *   Mechanism to identify incidence as soon as it occurs
*   Recovery of e-banking system and services
*   Communication strategy to address external parties and media
*   Procedure to alert related regulatory body~

column2: ~Why is Information Systems (IS) Audit important in banks, and how frequently should it be conducted?~
column3: ~IS audit is important in banks because the increasing complexity of IT environments creates significant risk. To ensure the effectiveness of implemented controls and the adequacy of security plans and procedures, banks should conduct IS audit annually.~

column2: ~What role does the board or audit committee play in conducting IS audits?~
column3: ~The board or the audit committee should provide sufficient resources to conduct the audit to ensure the audit team is capable of evaluating IT controls in sufficient IT coverage. If the bank doesn't have enough staff or lacks expertise, the audit committee should ensure that the outsourced service provider has expertise and experience in IS Audit.~

column2: ~What should a bank do if it lacks internal staff or expertise to perform an IS audit?~
column3: ~If a bank does not have enough staff to conduct an IS Audit or lacks expertise in its staff, it can outsource the IS audit to an external professional provider. However, the responsibility of audit planning, risk assessment, and follow-up remains with the bank. The audit committee should ensure that the outsourced service provider has expertise and experience in IS Audit.

column2: ~What responsibility does the audit committee have when IS audit is outsourced?~
column3: ~The audit committee should ensure that the outsourced service provider has expertise and experience in IS Audit. Additionally, the responsibility of audit planning, risk assessment, and follow up rests on the bank.~

column2: ~How does IS Audit contribute to managing IT-related risks in banks?~
column3: ~IS Audit contributes to managing IT-related risks in banks by ensuring the effectiveness of implemented controls framework and adequacy of the adopted security plan and procedures. Banks should conduct IS audit annually. Additionally, it helps in periodic risk assessment of their IT environment including human resource, technology, and processes, identifying probable events or activities that can adversely affect the bank's operation or reputation, and ensuring suitable mitigating strategies are in place.~

column2: ~Why is fraud management particularly important for Nepalese banks in the context of electronic delivery channels?~
column3: ~Nepalese banks are using electronic delivery channels (like internet banking, mobile banking, payment cards, and ATMs) increasingly. This increased usage creates a risk of electronic fraud in the banking system, making fraud management particularly important.~

column2: ~What is expected of banks regarding electronic attacks in their systems?~
column3: ~Banks should identify and document all electronic attacks and suspected electronic attacks in their system and report to Nepal Rastra Bank monthly.~

column2: ~How should banks educate customers about electronic fraud?~
column3: ~Banks should make customers aware of frauds along with fraud identification, avoidance, and protection measures.~

column2: ~Under which legal provisions has the Nepal Rastra Bank formulated the Money Changer Licensing and Inspection Bylaw, 2020?~
column3: ~According to the provided text, the Nepal Rastra Bank has formulated the Money Changer Licensing and Inspection Bylaw, 2020 under Section 63 of the Nepal Rastra Bank Act, 2002 and Section 3 of the Foreign Exchange (Regulation) Act, 1962.~

column2: ~When was the first amendment to the Nepal Rastra Bank Foreign Exchange Transaction Licensing and Inspection Bylaw, 2020 made?~
column3: ~The first amendment was made on December 2022 (meeting number: 31/2079).~

column2: ~When was the second amendment to the Nepal Rastra Bank Foreign Exchange Transaction Licensing and Inspection Bylaw, 2020 made??~
column3: ~The second amendment to the Nepal Rastra Bank Foreign Exchange Transaction Licensing and Inspection Bylaw, 2020 was made on November 28, 2023 (meeting number 28/2080).~

column2: ~What does the term “License” mean according to the Nepal Rastra Bank prepared the Foreign Exchange Transaction Licensing and Inspection Bylaw made?~
column3: ~"License" means the license issued by Rastra Bank to a firm, company or institution to get engaged in foreign exchange transaction in accordance with this Bylaw. However, separate license is to be obtained according to the Nepal Rastra Bank Money Changer Licensing and Inspection Bylaw, 2020 for money exchange transaction, and Nepal Rastra Bank Remittance Bylaw, 2023 for remittance transaction.

column2: ~Which separate licenses are required for money exchange and remittance transactions?~
column3: ~Separate license is to be obtained according to the Nepal Rastra Bank Money Changer Licensing and Inspection Bylaw, 2020 for money exchange transaction, and Nepal Rastra Bank Remittance Bylaw, 2023 for remittance transaction.~

column2: ~Who must obtain a license to carry out foreign exchange transaction?~
column3: ~Bank and financial institution, firm, company, institution and casino must obtain a license to carry out foreign exchange transaction.~

column2: ~How much licensing fee must a bank pay to obtain a foreign exchange transaction license?~
column3: ~A bank must pay a lump sum of five hundred thousand rupees to Rastra Bank to obtain a license for foreign exchange transactions.~

column2: ~What is the licensing fee for a national level development bank?~
column3: ~A lump sum of one hundred thousand rupees for a national level development bank.~

column2: ~From where shall financial institutions other than the national level be granted a license?~
column3: ~Financial institutions other than the national level shall be granted such license from the provincial office or department of Rastra Bank, located near its central office.

column2: ~What type of license shall be granted to licensed bank and financial institution?~
column3: ~License with no expiry date shall be granted to licensed bank and financial institution and such license shall not be required to renew.~

column2: ~Will any fee be charged for the new license issued after replacing the erstwhile license?~
column3: ~No fee shall be charged for such license if it's a replacement due to a merger or acquisition where the licensed bank or financial institution has undergone a merger or acquisition and has undertaken a new name.~

column2: ~What are the two procedures for Rastra Bank to issue a license to a firm, company or institution?~
column3: ~According to the provided text, Rastra Bank may issue a license to a firm, company or institution through these two procedures:

column1: a) By taking an application for license from the firm, company or institution intending to carry out foreign exchange transaction.
b) By inviting application of foreign exchange transaction for a specified place/region.~

column2: ~What financial condition disqualifies a person from being a director or office-bearer of an applying firm or company?~
column3: ~A person is disqualified if they:

column1: *   Are not of sound mental health.
*   Are falling under creditor's insolvency due to default of debt.
*   Have been blacklisted in any transaction with a bank or financial institution (unless at least three years have passed since release from the list).
*   Have been sentenced by a court for a criminal offense involving moral turpitude (unless five years have passed since serving the punishment).
*   Have been punished by the court for the offense of corruption or fraud.
*   Dishonesty or mal-intention has been proved for operation with a bank and financial institution.
*   Have been punished for crimes related to money laundering and financing of terrorism related activities.
*   If a criminal record is shown in the police report, five years have not elapsed after serving the sentence.~

column2: ~If a person was blacklisted in any transaction with a bank or financial institution, how long must have passed since release from the list to qualify?~
column3: ~At least three years must have passed from the date of release from the blacklist.~

column2: ~What offenses make a person ineligible to be a director or office-bearer under this bylaw?~
column3: ~A person is ineligible to be a director or office-bearer if they:

column1: *   Have not completed 18 years of age.
*   Do not have sound mental health.
*   Are falling under creditor's insolvency due to default of debt.
*   Are blacklisted in any transaction with a bank or financial institution (less than three years since release).
*   Have not paid taxes as per prevailing law.
*   Have been sentenced by a court for a criminal offense involving moral turpitude (less than five years since serving punishment).
*   Have been punished by a court for the offense of corruption or fraud.
*   Are holding a post in the service of any government, public or bank and financial institution.
*   Dishonesty or mal-intention has been proved for operation with a bank and financial institution.
*   Have been punished for crimes related to money laundering and financing of terrorism-related activities.
*   Have a criminal record shown in the police report (less than five years since serving the sentence).~

column2: ~Which types of firms or companies can accept payment in foreign exchange for sale of goods and services to foreign tourists?~
column3: ~Hotel, resort, travel agency, airlines, cargo company, trekking agency, and other firm, company, or institutions, or other service provider company and institutions can accept payment in foreign exchange for the sale of goods and services to foreign tourists, and persons, firms, companies, or institutions who can pay in foreign exchange in accordance with the Act.~

column2: ~What service can a hotel provide to its customers in relation to foreign currency?~
column3: ~A hotel can provide cash exchange of convertible foreign currency up to the limit specified by Rastra Bank. Additionally, licensed hotels may provide exchange facility of convertible foreign currency to their customers up to the limit set by Rastra Bank by issuing Foreign Exchange Encashment Receipts (FEER).

column2: ~How much bank guarantee is required for each place of transaction in case of departmental store, super market, airlines and hotel?~
column3: ~A bank guarantee equal to one hundred thousand rupees is required for each place of transaction for departmental stores, supermarkets, airlines, and hotels of three stars and above.~

column2: ~How much licensing fee shall a departmental store, supermarket, airline, or three-star and higher-level hotel pay per year for each place of transaction?~
column3: ~Four thousand rupees.~

column2: ~How much licensing fee shall other hotels, resorts, firms, companies, institutions, and government agencies pay per year for each place of transaction?~
column3: ~Other hotels, resorts, firms, companies, or institutions and the government agency shall pay licensing fee of two thousand rupees per year for each place of transaction.~

column2: ~To whom shall the licensing fee be paid to obtain a foreign currency transaction license?~
column3: ~The licensing fee shall be paid to Rastra Bank.~

column2: ~Who should a casino apply to for a license to undertake foreign exchange transaction?~
column3: ~A casino, willing to undertake foreign exchange transaction, should apply to the department or to the nearest provincial office of Rastra Bank.~

column2: ~In which format shall a casino submit the application for a foreign exchange transaction license?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, a casino willing to undertake foreign exchange transaction shall apply for a license in the format as per Schedule-4.~

column2: ~What qualification must a casino meet to apply for a foreign exchange transaction license?~
column3: ~The qualification of the director/office-bearer of the firm, company, or institution applying for a foreign exchange transaction must meet the following criteria:

column1: *   Has completed 18 years of age
*   Sound mental health
*   Not falling under creditor's insolvency due to default of debt
*   If blacklisted in any transaction with a bank or financial institution, at least three years must have passed from the date of release.
*   Have paid tax as per prevailing law
*   If sentenced by a court for a criminal offense involving moral turpitude, five years must have passed from serving such punishment.
*   Not having been punished by the court for the offense of corruption or fraud.
*   Not holding any post in the service of any government, public, or bank and financial institution.
*   Dishonesty or mal-intention has not been proved for operation with a bank and financial institution.
*   Not having been punished for crimes related to money laundering and financing of terrorism-related activities.
*   If a criminal record is shown in the police report, five years must have elapsed after serving the sentence.~

column2: ~What bank guarantee must a casino submit to obtain the license to transact in foreign currency?~
column3: ~A casino must submit a bank guarantee of two million five hundred thousand rupees having a validity of twenty-four months to obtain the license to transact in foreign currency. However, if the game is played through modern machine or equipment only, a bank guarantee of one million rupees having a validity of twenty-four months is required.~

column2: ~Under which laws and instructions must a licensed institution perform its tasks while transacting in foreign exchange?~
column3: ~Pursuant to the Act and the rules, bylaws and prevailing laws made thereunder and the circular, order, directive, notice by Rastra Bank from time to time and conditions mentioned while issuing the license, an institution licensed to transact in foreign exchange shall perform its tasks.~

column2: ~What must be done while accepting payment in local currency from foreign nationals?~
column3: ~While accepting local currency (Nepali rupees) from foreign nationals, the licensed institution shall accept it only up to the amount mentioned in FEER or receipt of ATM or POS machine. The overleaf of the FEER must be compulsorily endorsed, and its proof shall be kept.~

column2: ~Which laws must a licensed institution follow when dealing in foreign exchange?~
column3: ~When dealing in foreign exchange, the licensed institution must follow the prevailing laws regarding prevention of asset (money) laundering and combating the financing of terrorism related activities. Additionally, they must adhere to the Act and the rule, bylaw or circular, order and directive issued by Rastra Bank.

column2: ~What must a licensed institution provide to the inspectors appointed by Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~The licensed institution shall provide the details and documents requested by the inspectors appointed by Rastra Bank for inspection and also provide necessary assistance to the inspectors in inspection related work.~

column2: ~When can Rastra Bank inspect and supervise a licensed institution?~
column3: ~Rastra Bank can inspect and supervise a licensed institution at any time as required (Bylaw 18.1). Here are the different types of inspection and supervision:

column1: *   **Regular on-site inspection:** Rastra Bank may carry out this inspection.

*   **Special/emergency inspection:** This can be conducted if Rastra Bank realizes any irregularities, wrong information being provided, illegal transactions, or serious defects in submitted details.
*   **Targeted inspection:** Rastra Bank can carry out this inspection covering any specific transaction.
*   **Follow-up inspection:** This occurs if Rastra Bank wants assurance on submitted documents.~

column2: ~On what basis can Rastra Bank decide to conduct a special/emergency inspection?~
column3: ~Based on information received from public media, request/instruction/order from various government and other agencies, complaint and information received from other sources, Rastra Bank realizes that any licensed institution is committing irregularity, providing wrong information to general public, conducting illegal transaction, serious defects in the submitted details.~

column2: ~What irregularities may trigger a special/emergency inspection?~
column3: ~Based on the provided text, a special/emergency inspection may be triggered if Rastra Bank realizes that any licensed institution is committing irregularity, providing wrong information to the general public, conducting illegal transactions, or has serious defects in the submitted details. This realization can come from information received from public media, requests/instructions/orders from government agencies, complaints, or other sources.~

column2: ~What action may Rastra Bank take in case of violation of the regulation?~
column3: ~In case of a violation of regulation, Rastra Bank may impose one or more of the following punishments or fines on the licensed institution: giving reprimand, giving written warning, prohibiting any or all transactions in foreign exchange, confiscation of cash security, imposing a fine of up to one million rupees, suspension, or annulment of license. Additionally, Rastra Bank may write to the concerned institution for departmental action against directors, office-bearers or employees who have violated the regulations.~

column3: ~Answer: The inflation rate for the food and beverage category was 5.03 percent in mid-September 2024.~

column3: ~The inflation rate for non-food and services was 3.19 percent in the review month.~

column2: ~What was the balance of payments situation in mid-September 2024?~
column3: ~The balance of payments (BOP) remained at a surplus of Rs. 2152.53 billion in mid-September 2024. In US dollar terms, the BOP remained at a surplus of 16.04 billion.~
